claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 15 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 15 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is fostered but the council is not paying for accommodation or maintenance
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 15 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is fostered but the council is not paying for accommodation or maintenance; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child is fostered but the council is not paying for accommodation or maintenance
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! I'm here to help you check your Child Benefit eligibility. Let me start by asking about your child.

**Do you have a child or young person you're responsible for?**

If yes, please tell me:
1. **How old is your child?** (Or you can tell me their age category: under 16, 16-19, or 20 or over)
2. **What is their name?** (I'll use this to track their eligibility throughout our assessment)

This is important because Child Benefit eligibility primarily depends on the age of the child and whether they meet certain criteria based on that age.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'm responsible for a child. My son Alex is 15 years old. I wanted to check if I can claim Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! Let me assess the eligibility for your son Alex, who is 15 years old.

Since Alex is under 16, he falls into the automatic qualification age category. However, I need to check one important thing:

**Is Alex working full-time?**

By full-time, I mean working 24 hours or more per week in paid employment. This is important because children under 16 who work full-time are not considered qualifying children for Child Benefit purposes.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't work full-time at all. He's just focused on school right now.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Since Alex is not working full-time, that's good for eligibility purposes.

Now let me check the next requirement:

**Is Alex receiving certain benefits in his own right?**

Specifically, I'm asking whether Alex is receiving any of the following benefits:
- Income Support
- Income-based Jobseeker's Allowance
- Income-related Employment and Support Allowance
- Universal Credit
- Tax credits (Working Tax Credit or Child Tax Credit)

Children who receive these benefits in their own right are not considered qualifying children for Child Benefit.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't receive any benefits in his own name. He's only 15, so he doesn't get anything like that himself.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Since Alex doesn't receive any of those benefits in his own name, we can continue.

Now, the next question:

**Is Alex an apprentice?**

Specifically, I'm asking if Alex is on an advanced level apprenticeship (such as Level 3 or higher in England, or equivalent in Scotland, Wales, or Northern Ireland). Young people on advanced apprenticeships are not eligible for Child Benefit to be claimed for them.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't doing an apprenticeship. He's still in school at 15.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Since Alex isn't doing an apprenticeship and is still in school, that's all good.

Now I need to check some information about **you as the claimant**. 

**Do you live in the UK?**

To be eligible for Child Benefit, you generally need to be living in the UK. However, there are some exceptions for people living abroad in certain circumstances (such as Crown servants posted overseas, or certain situations within the EU/EEA).

So, do you currently live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK. We're based here in England.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Since you live in the UK, you meet the residency requirement.

Now let's check about your responsibility for Alex:

**Are you responsible for Alex?**

This means any of the following:
- Alex lives with you, OR
- You're contributing at least the Child Benefit rate (or the equivalent value) towards the cost of providing for Alex

In most cases, if Alex lives with you and you're his parent or guardian providing for him, you're considered responsible for him.

Do you meet this responsibility requirement for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, absolutely. Alex lives with me at home, and I'm his parent. I provide for all his needs.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Since Alex lives with you and you provide for him, you clearly meet the responsibility requirement.

Now, an important question about competing claims:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex, or is there anyone else who might also be eligible to claim for him?**

For example:
- Another parent or guardian
- Someone else Alex lives with part of the time
- Anyone else who contributes to his upkeep

If there's only you as the potential claimant, that's straightforward. But if there's another person who could also claim (like another parent), we need to consider who has priority.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, there's no one else claiming for Alex. It's just me - I'm the only one responsible for him and no one else is getting Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Since there's no other claimant, we can continue.

Now I need to check where Alex lives:

**Does Alex live in the UK?**

Generally, the child needs to be living in the UK to qualify for Child Benefit. However, there are some exceptions for children living abroad in certain circumstances (such as children of Crown servants posted overseas, or in certain educational situations abroad).

So, does Alex live in the UK with you?

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I see the system is now checking about Alex's living situation more specifically. Let me ask:

**Is Alex in any of these situations?**
1. Currently in hospital
2. In local authority care (looked after by the council)
3. Being fostered
4. Living normally at home with you

Based on what you've told me (that Alex lives with you at home), it sounds like he's living normally with you. Is that correct, or is there any special circumstance like hospital stay, care, or fostering?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Actually, I should mention - Alex is fostered. I'm fostering him, but the local authority doesn't give me any payments for his accommodation or maintenance. I'm doing it without financial support from the council.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that important detail! Since Alex is fostered, I need to check the specific arrangement.

**You mentioned that the local authority doesn't give you any payments for Alex's accommodation or maintenance. Let me confirm:**

Are you fostering Alex **without** receiving any payments from the local authority for his accommodation or maintenance? 

This is important because:
- If you're fostering and NOT receiving local authority payments, you CAN claim Child Benefit
- If you're fostering and ARE receiving local authority payments for his accommodation/maintenance, you generally cannot claim Child Benefit

Based on what you've said, it sounds like you're fostering without local authority payments, which means you should be able to claim. Is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's correct. I'm fostering Alex, but I don't get anything from the council to foster him. No payments for accommodation or maintenance at all.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Since you're fostering Alex without receiving local authority payments, you can proceed with claiming Child Benefit.

Now, let me check one more thing about local authority involvement:

**Is the local council paying you (or anyone else) towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

You've already told me the council isn't paying you, so I just want to make absolutely certain there are no council payments being made for Alex's care. Is that correct - no council payments at all?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right - no council payments at all. I'm covering everything myself without any financial support from the local authority.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Wonderful news! Based on all the information you've provided, let me now give you the final assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## 🎉 Excellent News - You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit! 

**For Alex (age 15):** ✅ **ELIGIBLE**

### Why You Qualify:

You meet all the Child Benefit eligibility requirements for Alex:

✅ **Alex's age:** At 15, he's under 16, which automatically qualifies  
✅ **Not working full-time:** He's not in paid work for 24+ hours per week  
✅ **No disqualifying benefits:** He doesn't receive benefits in his own right  
✅ **Not in a disqualifying apprenticeship:** He's in school, not on an apprenticeship  
✅ **Your residency:** You live in the UK  
✅ **Your responsibility:** Alex lives with you and you provide for him  
✅ **No competing claims:** No one else is claiming for Alex  
✅ **Fostering arrangement:** Crucially, because you're fostering Alex **without** receiving local authority payments for his accommodation or maintenance, you can claim Child Benefit

### Next Steps to Claim:

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** or adoption certificate
4. **Be aware:** If you or your partner earn over £60,000, you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit Charge (but you can still claim)
5. **Payments:** Made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

### How to Apply:

Visit: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

**Congratulations!** You should proceed with your Child Benefit application for Alex.

reasonings: Alex is eligible for Child Benefit. He is 15 years old (under 16, which is an automatically qualifying age). He is not working full-time (less than 24 hours per week). He does not receive any benefits in his own right. He is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship (he's still in school). The claimant lives in the UK and meets residency requirements. The claimant has responsibility for Alex as he lives with them and they provide for his needs. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. While Alex is fostered, the local authority is not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance, which means the foster carer can claim Child Benefit. All eligibility criteria are met.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex, who is a 15-year-old fostered child. Despite the fostering arrangement, eligibility is confirmed because the local authority is not providing any payments for Alex's accommodation or maintenance, and all other standard eligibility criteria are satisfied.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is eligible for Child Benefit. He is 15 years old (under 16, which is an automatically qualifying age). He is not working full-time (less than 24 hours per week). He does not receive any benefits in his own right. He is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship (he's still in school). The claimant lives in the UK and meets residency requirements. The claimant has responsibility for Alex as he lives with them and they provide for his needs. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. While Alex is fostered, the local authority is not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance, which means the foster carer can claim Child Benefit. All eligibility criteria are met.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex, who is a 15-year-old fostered child. Despite the fostering arrangement, eligibility is confirmed because the local authority is not providing any payments for Alex's accommodation or maintenance, and all other standard eligibility criteria are satisfied.
